skill.name: doc-coauthoring
skill.description: Guides users through a structured workflow for drafting, refining, and testing documentation with an AI coauthor.
skill.summary: Coauthor structured documentation through context gathering, section drafting, iterative edits, and reader testing.
skill.author: AstraBit-CPT

security_audit.summary: Static external-command and weak-crypto detections are false positives from Markdown prose, tool names, and ordinary workflow text. The remaining risk is legitimate but material: the skill can ask to read connected workspace content and create or edit documentation files, so publication should include a data-access warning.

medium_findings.title: Authorized Connector Access to Workspace Content
medium_findings.description: The workflow tells the assistant to fetch shared documents, team channels, and connector-backed context when integrations are available. This supports the skill purpose and includes user confirmation in places, but it can process sensitive organizational content if users grant broad access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly direct use of document and messaging integrations. The behavior appears legitimate and user-oriented, so the risk is data exposure through authorized access rather than malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Local Documentation File Creation and Editing
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to create Markdown files and replace section placeholders during drafting. This is expected for document coauthoring, but it modifies files in the working directory and should be limited to user-approved document paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file creation and editing instructions are direct and repeated. They do not show destructive behavior or command execution, but they are real filesystem actions.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown inline code references to assistant tools or filenames, such as create_file and str_replace. No shell command, Ruby execution, or user-controlled command construction is present at the cited locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows the backticks are Markdown formatting around tool names and file examples. There is no executable code block or shell invocation at these lines.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is a Benign Manual Testing Link
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points users to open a fresh Claude conversation for manual reader testing. It is not a programmatic network request and does not transmit data by itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears in numbered user instructions for manual testing. No code performs a request to the URL.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static high-risk weak-cryptography hits occur in prose lines about documentation, Claude, reader testing, and workflow steps. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing call, or security-sensitive implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain natural-language workflow content rather than code. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptography use.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Static Finding Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The cited line suggests one way for users to provide context by writing an information dump. It does not instruct scanning, probing, discovery, or network enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The phrase appears in documentation-writing guidance, not in operational security or network code. No reconnaissance behavior is described at the cited line.

content.user_title: Draft Stronger Documentation With Guided Coauthoring
content.value_statement: Important documents often fail because context stays in the author’s head. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through context gathering, section drafting, refinement, and reader testing.
actual_capabilities: Guides a three-stage workflow for context gathering, section refinement, and reader testing.
Asks targeted questions about document type, audience, desired impact, templates, and constraints.
Suggests document sections when the user does not already have a structure.
Creates a scaffold with section headers and placeholders when file or artifact tools are available.
Drafts and iteratively edits sections based on user selections and feedback.
Tests the finished document with a fresh Claude or manual reader-testing prompts.
limitations: It depends on user-provided context and connected tools for source material.
It does not verify factual claims, links, or technical details independently.
It may take longer than freeform drafting because it follows a staged process.
Reader testing is manual when sub-agent tools are not available.

use_cases.title: Draft a Technical Spec
use_cases.description: Turn scattered architecture notes, constraints, and trade-offs into a structured technical specification.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Decision Document
use_cases.description: Clarify a recommendation, capture alternatives, and test whether readers can understand the decision.

use_cases.title: Write a Proposal
use_cases.description: Build a proposal section by section, then refine it for audience fit and practical impact.

prompt_templates.title: Start a New Document
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin a guided writing session from a rough idea.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me use the doc coauthoring workflow to draft a technical spec. Ask me the context questions first.

prompt_templates.title: Turn Notes Into Structure
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert an information dump into an outline.
prompt_templates.prompt: I will paste rough notes for a proposal. Help me identify the audience, missing context, and a practical document structure.

prompt_templates.title: Refine One Section
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve a specific section through guided iteration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Work on the technical approach section with me. Ask clarifying questions, brainstorm points to include, then draft only that section.

prompt_templates.title: Run Reader Testing
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluate whether a completed document works for new readers.
prompt_templates.prompt: Test this document as a fresh reader. Predict realistic reader questions, identify ambiguity, and suggest targeted fixes.

output_examples.input: User wants to write a decision document but has only scattered notes.
output_examples.output: The assistant asks about audience, desired impact, constraints, and templates. It then proposes sections such as context, options, recommendation, risks, and rollout.

output_examples.input: User has drafted most sections and wants quality review.
output_examples.output: The assistant rereads the document for flow, redundancy, contradictions, unclear assumptions, and generic filler before suggesting focused revisions.

output_examples.input: User is ready to test a completed technical spec.
output_examples.output: The assistant generates reader questions, checks whether the document answers them, and lists gaps that should be fixed before sharing.

best_practices: Share audience, constraints, background, and known trade-offs before drafting begins.
Edit one section at a time so feedback can improve later sections.
Run reader testing before sharing the document with stakeholders.
anti_patterns: Skipping context gathering and asking for a polished final document immediately.
Using broad connector access without confirming which sources should be read.
Treating generated drafts as fact-checked without reviewing details yourself.

faq.question: What kinds of documents does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports technical specs, decision documents, proposals, RFCs, PRDs, and similar structured documents.

faq.question: Does it write the whole document at once?
faq.answer: No. It builds the document section by section so the user can guide structure, tone, and content.

faq.question: Can it use existing templates?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow asks for templates or existing documents and can use them when provided or accessible.

faq.question: Does it require connected tools?
faq.answer: No. Connected tools help gather context, but users can paste content directly instead.

faq.question: What is reader testing?
faq.answer: Reader testing checks whether a fresh reader can understand the document without the author’s hidden context.

faq.question: Is the skill safe for sensitive documents?
faq.answer: Use it only with approved sources and avoid connecting private channels or documents unless that access is intended.
